LAW AND MOTION 302, THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING. AS TO DEFENDANT INTERNATIONAL BUSINESS MACHINES, INC. AND SOFTLAYER TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'S MOTION TO STAY OR DISMISS PURSUANT TO CAL. CODE CIV. PROC. SECTION 410.30, THE MOTION TO STAY IS GRANTED. THE COURT GRANTS DEFENDANT'S MOTION TO STAY PURSUANT TO CCP SECTION 410.30 BECAUSE PLAINTIFF SIGNED A MASTER SERVICES AGREEMENT WHICH CONTAINED AN ENFORCEABLE FORUM SELECTION CLAUSE. calendar.matters:
CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar May-20-2016 Off Calendar

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Nov-23-2015 Continued To Jun-08-16 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Stay Or Dismiss Pursuant To Cal. Code Civ. Proc. Section 410.30
Off Calendar Oct-23-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. SUSANNE BERG, ESQ., Tel No. 415-407-1715 appearing for the Plaintiff;
Atty. JEFFREY RABKIN, ESQ. of Jones Day, Tel No. 415-626-3939 appearing for Defendants International Business Machines, Inc. and Softlayer Technologies, Inc.

RULING - A R G U E D - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. As to DEFENDANT INTERNATIONAL BUSINESS MACHINES, INC. AND SOFTLAYER TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'S Motion To Stay Or Dismiss Pursuant To Cal. Code Civ. Proc. Section 410.30, the motion to stay is granted. The court grants Defendant's motion to stay pursuant to CCP section 410.30 because Plaintiff signed a Master Services Agreement which contained an enforceable forum selection clause. The clause states that the exclusive venue for all disputes arising out of the MSA shall be in that courts in Westchester County, New York or federal district court for the southern district of New York. Plaintiff has failed to demonstrate that the clause is procedurally and substantively unconscionable. (See Smith Valentino & Smit, Inc. v. Superior Court (1976) 17 Cal.3d 491, 496; Bernstein v. Wysoki (N.Y. App.Div. 2010) 77 A.D.3d 241, 248-49.) The clause was in the same font size as the rest of the MSA's terms and it was under a bolded heading entitled, "Governing Law, Lawsuits." Although SoftLayer is a subsidiary of IBM Company, SoftLayer regularly advertises itself as an IBM Company, which is a New York company. (Complaint paragraph 8.) Thus, in signing the MSA with SoftLayer, Plaintiff accepted the prospect of litigating in New York as "part of the price of doing business" with a New York company. (Cal-State Business Prods. & Servs, Inc. v. Ricoh (1993) 12 Cal.App.4th 1666, 1681.) Furthermore, Plaintiff has failed to cite to any non-waivable statutory rights that are not available in the chosen forum. (See Am. Online, Inc. v. Superior Court (2001) 90 Cal.App.4th 1.) ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Linda S. Forster, CSR #13286. (302/EHG)
